mnemonics.title: KISS - Knowledge, Inherence, Possession, Location
mnemonics.content: Authentication factors: K - Something you Know (passwords), I - Something you Inhere/Biometric (fingerprint), P - Something you Possess (token), L - Something you do/Location (behavior/location)

mnemonics.title: FIDO - Fast Identity Online
mnemonics.content: FIDO Alliance promotes passwordless authentication using public-key cryptography; Remember: FIDO fights password fatigue

mnemonics.title: AAA - Authentication, Authorization, Accounting
mnemonics.content: The security triangle: A - Confirming identity, A - Granting permissions, A - Tracking actions for audit

mnemonics.title: Kerberos Protocol Steps - "Peter's Ticket Grants Access"
mnemonics.content: P - Pre-authentication, T - Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT), G - Granting service tickets, A - Access to resources

mnemonics.title: OAuth 2.0 Flow - "RASCAL"
mnemonics.content: R - Request, A - Authorize, S - Send code, C - Code exchange, A - Access token, L - Login complete

acronyms.usage: Use when combining 2+ authentication methods (password + OTP, fingerprint + PIN) for enhanced security
Use when one set of credentials grants access to multiple related systems (Google login for YouTube, Gmail, Drive)
Use for temporary, single-use codes sent via SMS, email, or authenticator apps (e.g., bank transaction verification)
Use for rotating codes that change every 30 seconds (Google Authenticator, Authy apps)

acronyms.expansion: Completely Automated Public Turing test to tell Computers and Humans Apart
acronyms.usage: Use to differentiate humans from bots during authentication (login, registration, voting)
Use for certificate-based authentication using asymmetric key pairs and digital certificates
Use for SSO between identity providers and service providers in XML format
Use for delegated access (login with Google/Facebook without sharing passwords)
Use for authentication layer on top of OAuth 2.0 for user identity verification

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a three-lock treasure chest: Lock 1 = PIN (knowledge), Lock 2 = Key card (possession), Lock 3 = Fingerprint scanner (inherence). All three must unlock to access.

Picture a medieval castle: Client = Knight, AS = Gatekeeper (Authentication Server), TGS = Ticket Booth (Ticket Granting Server), Service = Treasury. Knight gets temporary passes from booth to enter treasury.

Think of valet parking: You give your ID (authorization request) → Hotel gives you a claim ticket (code) → You exchange ticket for car (access token) → Valet (resource server) lets you drive (access resource).
Imagine a mailbox: Everyone has the mailbox (public key) to drop messages in, but only you have the mailbox key (private key) to open and read them.
Think of your fingerprint/face as a unique signature key that cannot be borrowed, lost, or forgotten - it's permanently 'possessed' by you.

analogies.analogy: Like a door key - something you know and carry in your memory. Vulnerable if copied (key copied) or guessed (lock picked).
Like an ID badge or hotel key card - something you physically possess. Lose it = someone else can access. Like losing your house keys.
Like recognizing a friend's face - inherent, cannot be transferred, always with you. But like a face, cannot be changed if compromised.
Like needing both a PIN and a physical ATM card to withdraw money - two different types of proof needed, not just two passwords.
Like a master key that opens all doors in a hotel - one key for room, pool, gym, parking instead of carrying multiple keys.
Like giving a valet your car but not giving them ownership - they can drive it (access) but cannot sell it (use your data elsewhere).
Like a movie theater: Ticket counter (TGS) gives you show-specific tickets (service tickets) for a limited time, rather than one pass for all shows.
Like a government-issued passport - trusted third party (passport office) verifies your identity, and others trust it without needing to verify you themselves.
